name: multiturn_benchmark_v1
description: Authored short multi-turn benchmark for follow-up resolution, comparison carryover, and compact conversational continuity.
notes: This benchmark is product-facing rather than judge-graded.
Use it to inspect whether the system preserves comparison axes, referenced entities, and branch context across turns.
This manifest does not encode automatic pass or fail rules.

cases.question: What about under each one, when is Canada legally bound?
cases.description: Two-turn comparison follow-up that should preserve standing-offer versus supply-arrangement context.

messages.content: What is the actual contractual difference between a standing offer and a supply arrangement?
A standing offer is not itself a contract, while a supply arrangement is also not a contract but a framework for future solicitations.
What about under each one, when is Canada legally bound?

cases.question: What if it came through CPC Connect instead?
cases.description: Two-turn workflow follow-up that should preserve the delayed-offer context and answer the CPC Connect branch.

messages.content: We received a bid after the deadline and the supplier says they sent it on time. What proof counts, and what are we supposed to do with the offer?
You need acceptable proof of timely receipt or delayed-offer evidence. Otherwise the late offer is returned or rejected.
What if it came through CPC Connect instead?

cases.question: And which part actually tells me that?
cases.description: Three-turn source-authority follow-up that should preserve the current topic and answer as a navigation/source question rather than restarting retrieval broadly.

messages.content: The Buy Canadian policy page sounds stricter than the Buyer's Guide. Can I just rely on the policy page and skip the Buyer's Guide reciprocal procurement workflow?
No. The policy page and the Buyer's Guide workflow are doing different jobs, and the Buyer's Guide still governs the reciprocal procurement workflow.
And which part actually tells me that?

cases.question: Does that change if the work has security requirements?
cases.description: Three-turn follow-up that should preserve the method-of-supply comparison and add the security branch without dropping the original context.

messages.content: When should I use a standing offer instead of a supply arrangement?

A standing offer fits repeated needs for well-defined goods or services with preset pricing, while a supply arrangement is used when future solicitations will still be needed among a pool of suppliers.
Does that change if the work has security requirements?
